digest.top_line: NRG/RTOG 1112: adding SBRT to sorafenib mOS 15.8 vs 12.3mo (HR 0.77, 1-sided P=.06, ns 1°); stratification-adjusted OS HR 0.72 P=.04, mPFS 9.2 vs 5.5mo.
digest.tldr: Two RT-forward reads. RTOG 1112 gives SBRT its first randomised PFS signal (HR 0.55) added to sorafenib in locally advanced HCC, though 1° OS just missed and the sorafenib comparator is now obsolete. Alongside, a ReCOG/ESTRO/ASTRO consensus formalizes reRT selection for recurrent HNSCC on a thin, mostly retrospective base.

sites.intro: Guidance where randomised data doesn't exist: a formal reRT consensus for recurrent/2nd-primary HNSCC in previously irradiated fields.

studies.name: ReCOG/ESTRO/ASTRO HNSCC Reirradiation Consensus
studies.tldr: Expert consensus on reRT for recurrent/2nd-primary HNSCC in previously irradiated fields; 17-panel vote, guidance on selection, delineation, dose accumulation, toxicity.
details: 🔍 Multi-society consensus (ReCOG + ESTRO Reirradiation Focus Group + ASTRO) on reRT for recurrent or 2nd-primary HNSCC in previously irradiated fields
🔍 Method: 10-expert core draft → single formal vote by an international panel of 17 radiation oncologists (agree/disagree/no-opinion)
📐 Consensus thresholds predefined: high ≥85%, moderate 70-84%, low <70%; no-opinion retained in the denominator, no second voting round
🔍 Domains covered: patient selection, imaging, target delineation, treatment planning, dose accumulation, toxicity management
🔍 Applies to definitive and postoperative reRT; salvage surgery preferred when feasible, then postop reRT for high-risk pts
📊 15-50% of HNSCC pts recur or develop a 2nd primary; few options once disease is in-field
🔗 Modern conformal technique (IMRT, VMAT, proton, SBRT) has widened reRT feasibility over ~20 yrs, but modern-era role remains undefined
⚠️ Low-level evidence base: most statements rest on Oxford level 3-4 (retrospective / expert consensus), not randomised data
⚠️ Excludes nasopharyngeal cancers (2021 recs) and rare histologies
studies.significance: The dose-accumulation and target-delineation domains are the real value: reRT toxicity is gated by cumulative OAR dose from the first course, and this is the first multi-society attempt to standardize how you sum the two plans and draw the reRT target. It sequences salvage surgery first, then postoperative or definitive reRT, so it shapes selection, not a dose prescription.
studies.monday_clinic: In a pt with an in-field HNSCC recurrence or second primary (non-nasopharyngeal) being weighed for reRT, this frames selection, dose accumulation, and delineation; it does not extend to first-course or nasopharyngeal disease.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: **Policy Review / expert-consensus statement.** A 10-member core (6 radiation oncologists, 3 physicists, 1 research fellow) drafted, then an international panel of **17 radiation oncologists** cast a single formal vote. Consensus predefined as **high ≥85%, moderate 70-84%, low <70%**, with no-opinion kept in the denominator.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Recurrent or second-primary **HNSCC within a previously irradiated region**, a setting with scarce options. Background: **15-50%** of HNSCC pts recur or develop a second primary.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: Covers **definitive and postoperative reRT** with modern conformal technique (IMRT, VMAT, proton, SBRT). Guidance spans patient selection, imaging, target delineation, treatment planning, **dose accumulation**, and toxicity management; salvage surgery preferred when feasible.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents recurrent or second-primary HNSCC arising in a previously irradiated region. Does not represent nasopharyngeal carcinoma, rare histologies, or first-course HNSCC.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Evidence base is low-level (much at Oxford **level 3-4**, retrospective / expert consensus); **single voting round** with no reconciliation, and a radiation-oncologist-only panel.

open_questions: Role of modern reRT (IMRT/proton/SBRT) undefined absent randomised trials
Optimal dose accumulation method across two RT courses
Selection for definitive vs postoperative reRT

sites.intro: SBRT's first randomised OS/PFS signal in locally advanced HCC, but the sorafenib-alone comparator predates current IO-based 1L.

studies.name: NRG/RTOG 1112
studies.tldr: Adding SBRT to sorafenib: mOS 15.8 vs 12.3mo (HR 0.77, 1-sided P=.06, ns primary); mPFS 9.2 vs 5.5mo, HR 0.55, P<.001.

details.text: 📊 SBRT+sorafenib vs sorafenib alone, by endpoint

details: 📐 Stratification-adjusted OS reached significance: HR 0.72 (95% CI 0.52-0.99), 2-sided P=.04
📐 SBRT personalized to 27.5-50 Gy in 5 fractions, liver-function adapted, delivered before sorafenib
🔍 Phase 3 open-label RCT, 193 randomized (177 eligible), 1:1 SBRT→sorafenib vs sorafenib
🔍 Pts: locally advanced HCC unsuitable for or refractory to locoregional therapy; 74% macrovascular invasion; 1L systemic candidates
⚠️ Primary OS missed prespecified significance (1-sided P=.06); benefit rests on adjusted OS plus PFS
⚠️ Accrual stopped early after 1L systemic SOC shifted; sorafenib-alone comparator now obsolete
📊 6-mo QoL improved in 6/17 (35%) SBRT+sorafenib vs 2/20 (10%) sorafenib

verdict.rationale: Randomised phase 3 argues for adding SBRT to systemic in MVI-heavy HCC, but primary OS not significant (1-sided P=.06), stopped early, sorafenib comparator obsolete.
verdict.audience: Locally advanced HCC, macrovascular invasion (74%), 1L systemic candidates

studies.significance: The RT read is PFS, not the ns OS primary: mPFS 9.2 vs 5.5mo (HR 0.55, P<.001), a locoregional-control signal in a cohort 74% macrovascular-invasion, where liver-directed RT is hardest. Personalized 27.5-50Gy/5fx, no excess G3+ toxicity. Open decision: does SBRT still add over a modern IO backbone (sorafenib obsolete)?
studies.monday_clinic: In locally advanced HCC with macrovascular invasion refractory to or unsuitable for standard locoregional therapy, this supports adding SBRT to first-line systemic as contested evidence; it does not establish benefit over a modern IO-based first-line backbone.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Phase 3 open-label RCT, 1:1, **193 randomized (177 eligible)**, stratified by performance status, liver function, degree of metastases, and macrovascular invasion. Accrual stopped early after first-line systemic SOC shifted.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Locally advanced HCC **unsuitable for or refractory to standard locoregional therapy**, fit for first-line systemic. 84.7% male, median age 66; **macrovascular invasion in 74%**.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: **Personalized SBRT, 27.5 to 50 Gy in 5 fractions**, dose adapted to liver function, delivered before sorafenib.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary: overall survival.** Secondary: progression-free survival, adverse events, quality of life.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Primary OS trend favored SBRT but **missed the prespecified 1-sided threshold** (P=.06); stratification-adjusted OS and the secondary PFS were both significant. See the endpoint table.

analysis_sections.label: Safety
analysis_sections.body: G3+ treatment-related AEs similar (**47% vs 42%**, P=.52). Treatment-related deaths: 2 with sorafenib (liver failure, death NOS), 1 with SBRT+sorafenib (lung infection). **No excess RT-attributable toxicity.**

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: Predates modern first-line combinations (atezolizumab-bevacizumab, durvalumab-tremelimumab); the sorafenib-alone comparator is now obsolete, leaving open whether **SBRT adds over an IO backbone**.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents locally advanced HCC with macrovascular invasion, unsuitable for or refractory to locoregional therapy, fit for first-line systemic. Does not represent early-stage resectable or ablatable HCC, or patients on modern IO-based first-line systemic therapy.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Open-label; **accrual stopped early** (underpowered); primary OS not significant unadjusted; comparator superseded; QoL assessed in small subsets (n=17-20).

open_questions: Does SBRT add over modern IO-based first-line systemic therapy?
Optimal SBRT dose and fractionation with impaired liver function

papers.title: Stereotactic Body Radiotherapy vs Sorafenib Alone in Hepatocellular Carcinoma: The NRG Oncology/RTOG 1112 Phase 3 Randomized Clinical Trial.
papers.abstract:
IMPORTANCE: Most patients with locally advanced hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) recur within the liver following systemic therapy.

OBJECTIVE: To determine whether stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT) improves outcomes in patients with locally advanced HCC compared with sorafenib alone.

DESIGN, SETTING, AND PARTICIPANTS: This multicenter phase 3 randomized clinical trial randomized patients with HCC 1:1 to sorafenib or SBRT followed by sorafenib, stratified by performance status, liver function, degree of metastases, and macrovascular invasion. Eligible patients had HCC unsuitable for or refractory to standard local-regional therapies and were candidates for first-line systemic therapy. Data were collected from April 2013 to March 2021, and data were analyzed from July 2022 to August 2023.

INTERVENTION: Personalized SBRT, 27.5 to 50 Gy in 5 fractions.

MAIN OUTCOMES AND MEASURES: The primary end point was overall survival (OS). Secondary end points were progression-free survival (PFS), adverse events, and quality of life.

RESULTS: Of 193 patients randomized, 177 were eligible. Accrual was stopped early due to a change in standard-of-care systemic therapy. Of 177 included patients, 150 (84.7%) were male, and the median (IQR) age was 66 (60-72) years. Macrovascular invasion was seen in 131 (74.0%). As of July 1, 2022, the median OS was 12.3 months (90% CI, 10.6-14.3) with sorafenib vs 15.8 months (90% CI, 11.4-19.2) following SBRT and sorafenib (hazard ratio [HR], 0.77; 90% CI, 0.59-1.01; 1-sided P = .06). Adjusting for stratification factors, OS was improved with SBRT (HR, 0.72; 95% CI, 0.52-0.99; 2-sided P = .04). Median PFS was improved from 5.5 months (95% CI, 3.4-6.3) with sorafenib to 9.2 months (95% CI, 7.5-11.9) with SBRT and sorafenib (HR, 0.55; 95% CI, 0.40-0.75; 2-sided P < .001). Treatment-related grade 3 or higher adverse events were seen in 37 of 88 (42%) and 39 of 83 (47%) of patients treated with sorafenib vs SBRT and sorafenib, respectively (P = .52). There were 2 treatment-related deaths in the sorafenib group (death not otherwise specified and liver failure) and 1 in the SBRT and sorafenib group (lung infection). At 6 months, improved quality of life was seen in 2 of 20 (10%) and 6 of 17 (35%) of patients treated with sorafenib and SBRT and sorafenib, respectively.

CONCLUSIONS AND RELEVANCE: In this phase 3 randomized clinical trial, among patients with locally advanced HCC, SBRT was associated with a clinically important but not statistically significant improved overall survival compared with sorafenib alone.

TRIAL REGISTRATION: ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: NCT01730937.

papers.title: Reirradiation for recurrent head and neck squamous cell carcinoma: international expert consensus recommendations endorsed by the Reirradiation Collaborative Group, the European Society for Radiotherapy and Oncology Reirradiation Focus Group, and the American Society for Radiation Oncology
